segments.title: Turning non-laboratory evidence into analytical writing
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke opens the advanced methods lesson by connecting research design to paragraph structure and warning that evidence earns little unless the writer analyzes and links it into a coherent argument.
segments.body: The lesson begins with the practical relationship between gathering evidence and presenting an argument. Dr. Clarke uses the statement-evidence-analysis-link structure to show that a large body of research does not speak for itself. Marks and intellectual value depend on explaining what the evidence means and connecting each paragraph to the larger claim. He describes the final write-up as a small-looking but decisive part of the project because weak analysis can squander extensive earlier work. Opening here gives viewers a compact statement of why methodology and writing structure must be planned together.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke introduces non-laboratory research and explains why evidence must be analyzed and linked rather than merely listed.

segments.title: Literature studies, systematic reviews, and primary research
segments.summary: Different departments may label a literature study as a systematic, systemic, thematic, or prior-research review, but the dissertation still needs enough evidence and a defensible contribution of primary research.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares several overlapping names used for evidence-led reviews and argues that students should concentrate on the required work rather than become distracted by terminology. At bachelor’s and master’s level, the central task is to assemble a substantial evidence base and identify what counts as the project’s primary or original research. Losing access to a laboratory changes the available methods but does not remove the need for a defensible research contribution. He also distinguishes degree levels mainly through the expected depth of critical analysis rather than a wholly different standard of evidence. This watch point helps viewers translate departmental labels into the underlying methodological requirements.

evidence.note: The lecture compares names for literature-led reviews, discusses primary research, and distinguishes bachelor’s from master’s work by analytical depth.

segments.title: Combining literature, case studies, interviews, and surveys
segments.summary: Several viable dissertation designs combine a literature study with case studies, interviews, or surveys, using interviews for qualitative depth and surveys for larger quantities of comparable data.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke lays out alternative method combinations for projects that cannot rely on laboratory work. A literature study supplies context and maps the existing debate, while case studies bring evidence together around particular events, objects, or problems. Interviews can provide original qualitative material whose value lies in detailed answers, whereas surveys can produce a much larger quantitative dataset suitable for charts and comparisons. These methods can be combined in different proportions according to the question and the researcher’s strengths. The section is useful for seeing research design as a coordinated evidence system rather than a list of disconnected techniques.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke compares combinations of literature studies, case studies, interviews, and surveys, including qualitative and quantitative outputs.

segments.title: A naval historian’s dissertation method in practice
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke contrasts a wholly archival dissertation with another project that used interviews and tightly focused amphibious-operation case studies to investigate a particular war.
segments.body: The abstract method choices become concrete through Dr. Clarke’s own training as a naval historian. One dissertation depended entirely on archival research, while another combined a literature study, interviews, and detailed case studies of amphibious operations within a war. The case studies narrowed a large conflict into specific events that could be examined in depth without losing the broader historical setting. He presents this as a familiar historical and social-science approach that can also help science or engineering students when laboratory access is limited. Opening the video here shows how a research question determines the useful mix of sources and methods.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes his archival dissertation and a naval-history project built around interviews and amphibious-operation case studies.

segments.title: Building a balanced literature study across source types
segments.summary: A literature study can be organized by research objectives while deliberately alternating journal articles, books, and magazines, then giving each source more analytical space than a conventional literature review.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke proposes a source portfolio that mixes journal articles, books, and magazines rather than allowing one format to dominate. The material should be grouped under the dissertation’s objectives or subtopics so that every discussion serves the central question. Where a normal literature review may devote one or two paragraphs to a work, the expanded literature study uses at least three analytical paragraphs. That extra space is intended for context, findings, and evaluation rather than plot-like summary. The watch point offers a practical architecture for turning broad reading into an ordered critical chapter.

evidence.note: The lecture recommends mixing source types, grouping them by dissertation objectives, and expanding each source discussion beyond a standard literature review.

segments.title: Testing provenance, results, and conclusions
segments.summary: A three-part source assessment asks who produced and funded the work, what the evidence actually shows, and how much value the source has for the dissertation’s wider argument.
segments.body: The first analytical paragraph should establish where, when, why, and by whom the research was conducted. Dr. Clarke emphasizes that a government, university, commercial company, or opaquely funded think tank may bring different incentives and limitations to the evidence. The next task is to compare the reported results with the claims made in the conclusion, since confident conclusions can exceed what the data supports. A final paragraph assesses the work from the perspective of the entire dissertation rather than only one narrow objective. This framework is directly useful for historical source criticism because it joins provenance, internal consistency, and relevance in one repeatable test.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke sets out three analytical paragraphs covering provenance and funding, findings versus claims, and value to the wider dissertation.

segments.title: Academic voice and the tradeoffs of a fixed word count
segments.summary: Critical judgment can be expressed in impersonal academic language, while every extra paragraph must justify the space it takes from another part of a dissertation’s fixed word count.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that academic judgment need not be written through first-person phrases such as ‘I think.’ Formulations such as ‘the balance of evidence would suggest’ allow the writer to state a reasoned position while keeping the emphasis on the argument. He then returns to the three-paragraph source pattern and notes that synthesis paragraphs may be added to tie a group of works together. Because the word count is finite, additional analysis in the literature study reduces the space available elsewhere and must earn its place. The section links style and structure to the larger problem of allocating attention across a research project.

evidence.note: The lecture discusses impersonal academic phrasing, section synthesis, and balancing analytical depth against a fixed word count.

segments.title: Choosing viable case studies and recording every source
segments.summary: A useful case study must be relevant, well documented, and realistically accessible, while exhaustive source records protect a deeply immersed researcher from accidental plagiarism.
segments.body: Case selection begins with relevance to the research question, but relevance alone is insufficient. Dr. Clarke argues that enough material must exist and that the researcher must be able to reach it under real constraints, including closed archives, travel restrictions, and interview access. He recommends several cases when the project seeks comparison, while recognizing that intensive study can make the researcher highly familiar with each subject. That familiarity creates a citation risk because remembered wording or ideas may become detached from their source. Recording everything consulted in the bibliography provides an essential audit trail and a defense against accidental plagiarism.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke defines relevant, well-supported, viable case studies and warns researchers to record every source as their familiarity deepens.

segments.title: Single-case and comparative case-study designs
segments.summary: A single large case can support an entire dissertation when backed by extensive surveys and interviews, while multiple smaller cases create opportunities for structured comparison.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke illustrates the single-case approach with a dissertation on the Jubilee Line’s construction and its effects on residents above the London Underground works. The project combined a literature study with thousands of survey responses and roughly fifteen to twenty interviews, making one infrastructure programme large enough to sustain the whole dissertation. Multiple-case designs work differently by comparing several bridges, retailers, therapies, games, or other bounded subjects. Comparison can reveal differences that a solitary case would leave invisible, but each case still requires adequate evidence. Opening here helps viewers match the number of cases to the scale and purpose of their evidence collection.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts a survey- and interview-rich Jubilee Line dissertation with comparative multi-case designs.

segments.title: Historical context and integrated case-study evidence
segments.summary: Case studies need enough chronological context to judge what matters, and their chapters should integrate interviews or survey results with literature rather than isolate each method in a separate block.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests looking widely around the date of a historical subject so that the researcher can decide which earlier and later developments actually matter. The surrounding material may not all appear in the final text, but it enables informed selection and prevents the case from being treated in isolation. A proposed combined railway crossing and second Thames Barrier demonstrates how bridge types, flood defenses, and transport planning can become linked research contexts. He then explains that interview or survey findings should be woven into the relevant case-study chapters alongside the literature. The result is an integrated explanation of each case rather than separate piles of evidence organized only by collection method.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke discusses broad chronological context, a railway and Thames Barrier example, and integrating primary data into each case-study chapter.

segments.title: Leading questions, bias, and the interpretation of polls
segments.summary: Survey and interview questions must be fair, consistent, and neutral because leading phrasing can manufacture the desired response and invalidate both a student project and public polling data.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces questionnaires and interviews as ethically demanding forms of public interaction. A researcher can steer an answer simply by changing the wording of a question, even when the resulting statistics look persuasive. That influence introduces bias into the data and may invalidate the conclusions. The same caution applies when reading political or public-opinion polling because the answer can reflect the question’s framing as much as the underlying circumstances. This watch point gives viewers a practical source-critical reason to inspect the instrument behind any reported survey result.

evidence.note: The lecture explains how leading questions introduce bias and why raw polling data must be read in light of the questions asked.

segments.title: Standardized interviews and verifiable transcripts
segments.summary: Interviewees should receive the same core questions, with follow-ups allowed, and a timed transcript should preserve who said what so the original evidence can be checked and cited.
segments.body: Consistency makes interview evidence comparable across participants. Dr. Clarke therefore requires the same core questions for everyone while allowing follow-up questions when an answer needs clarification or development. A full transcript should record the speakers, wording, and timing of the exchange. Placing that record in the appendices preserves the primary evidence without consuming the main word count. The researcher can then cite the interview transcript much like another documentary source, allowing readers and markers to verify the interpretation.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke requires consistent core interview questions and complete timed transcripts that can be placed in appendices and referenced.

segments.title: Survey deployment, privacy, and sample size
segments.summary: Online surveys can collect data while the researcher handles other commitments, but the platform must protect respondents and optional contact details must remain a deliberate choice.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the scheduling demands of interviews with the ability to leave an online survey open for several weeks. A survey platform can continue collecting responses while the researcher manages work or family commitments, especially when the link circulates through social media. Privacy remains central: the system should protect both researcher and respondent data, and identifying information should be requested only through an explicit optional invitation for follow-up. Larger response pools make it easier to identify anomalous or unserious entries and reduce their effect on the findings. The section joins project management, consent, and statistical resilience in one practical discussion.

evidence.note: The lecture compares survey and interview scheduling, discusses optional contact information and privacy, and explains the value of more responses.

segments.title: Researcher neutrality when the subject matters deeply
segments.summary: Passion for a topic cannot be allowed to override respondents’ answers: the researcher’s job is to listen, preserve what people say, and avoid inserting preferred conclusions into their evidence.
segments.body: The closing methodological warning focuses on the researcher’s own investment in the subject. Strong interest can help sustain a project, but it can also tempt an interviewer to lead participants or replace their answers with the researcher’s preferred interpretation. Dr. Clarke distinguishes academic evidence gathering from an exchange in which the questioner tries to persuade or instruct the respondent. Good interviews and surveys require listening and allowing participants to state their own views. Without that neutrality, the project loses both data quality and ethical credibility.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke warns researchers not to let passion for a topic lead respondents or put words into their mouths.
